papers.title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from Gaia hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: F. A. Evans T. Marchetti E. M. Rossi

abstract.text: Following a dynamical encounter with Sgr A*, binaries in the Galactic Centre (GC) can be tidally separated and one member star ejected as a hyper-velocity star (HVS) with a velocity beyond the escape speed of the Milky Way. As GC-born objects located in more observationally accessible regions of the sky, HVSs offer insight into the stellar population in the inner parsecs of the Milky Way. We perform a suite of simulations ejecting stars from the GC, exploring how detectable HVS populations depend on assumptions concerning the GC stellar population, focusing on HVSs which would appear in current and/or future data releases from the \\\\textit{Gaia} space mission with precise astrometry and measured radial velocities. We show that predictions are sensitive to two parameters in particular: the shape of the stellar initial mass function (IMF) in the GC and the ejection rate of HVSs. The absence of confident HVS candidates in \\\\textit{Gaia} Data Release 2 excludes scenarios in which the HVS ejection rate is $\\\\gtrsim3\\\\times10^{-2} \\\\, \\\\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$. Stricter constraints will be placed on these parameters when more HVS candidates are unearthed in future \\\\textit{Gaia} data releases -- assuming recent determinations of the GC IMF shape, one confident HVS \\\\textit{at minimum} is expected in \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 and DR4 as long as the HVS ejection rate is greater than $\\\\sim 10^{-3} \\\\, \\\\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$ and $\\\\sim10^{-5} \\\\, \\\\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$, respectively.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper uses dynamical simulations to model the ejection of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from the Galactic Centre via tidal disruption of binaries during encounters with Sgr A*. It shows that the observed absence of confident HVS candidates in Gaia Data Release 2 strongly constrains the HVS ejection rate to be less than $3 \times 10^{-2} \, \mathrm{yr^{-1}}$, and under current IMF assumptions, at least one HVS is expected in Gaia DR3/DR4 if the ejection rate exceeds $10^{-3} \, \mathrm{yr^{-1}}$ (DR3) or $10^{-5} \, \mathrm{yr^{-1}}$ (DR4), providing a direct probe of the stellar population and binary dynamics in the inner parsec of the Milky Way.

source_fields.title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from Gaia hypervelocity stars

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The experiment finds that Gaia DR2 is expected to detect fewer than one high-confidence hypervelocity star (HVS) under typical ejection rates, while DR3 and DR4 are predicted to detect at least one HVS for ejection rates above $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-5}\\,\\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$ respectively, with DR4 potentially detecting tens of thousands of HVSs under optimistic assumptions, providing strong constraints on the Galactic Centre ejection parameters $\\kappa$, $\\pi$, and $\\gamma$. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method using hyper-velocity stars as a back-door tracer to overcome observational challenges in the Galactic Centre, enabling the study of the young stellar cluster and nuclear star cluster's properties through their ejected former companions. Hills Ejection Model: The paper proposes a Monte Carlo model of the Hills mechanism in the Galactic Center that generates populations of hypervelocity stars by sampling binary properties—mass, mass ratio, and orbital period—from power-law distributions to simulate ejection velocities and masses, with the core idea being to reproduce realistic stellar ejection statistics based on observed binary population distributions and dynamical constraints. Discussion \\& Conclusions: Future Gaia DR3 and DR4 HVS detections will primarily constrain the nuclear star cluster's initial mass function and the HVS ejection rate, with population sizes showing strong dependence on these parameters. Existing Galactic Centre Constraints: This section outlines the observational and theoretical basis for the assumed initial mass function and binary properties in the Galactic Centre, emphasizing top-heavy mass functions and constraints from stellar observations and binary statistics. Appendix A The properties of the Gaia HVS population: The Gaia HVS population predicted by the fiducial model shows a concentration near the Galactic Centre, with DR4 HVSs extending up to ∼15 kpc and preferentially located at low Galactic latitudes near the meridian, due to dust attenuation and ejection dynamics. Appendix B Contamination by unbound stars not ejected from the GC: The vast majority of predicted Gaia DR4 hypervelocity stars can be reliably traced back to the Galactic Center, with 81% of their disc-crossing locations within 1 kpc and 96% within 2 kpc, indicating minimal contamination from unbound stars not ejected from the GC.

title: Constraints on the Galactic Centre environment from Gaia hypervelocity stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents theoretical simulations of HVS ejection from Galactic Centre, uses absence of HVS candidates in Gaia DR2 to constrain ejection rates and IMF. No new observational data or catalog presented.
